## Security & Compliance

> ⚠️ LOCKED — These rules cannot be overridden.

### Injection Prevention

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Parameterized queries only
NEVER concatenate user input into SQL/NoSQL/LDAP queries. MUST use parameterized queries (`$1`, `?`, `:param`), prepared statements, or ORM bindings. Example: `query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [id])` not `query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ' + id)`. Applies to all query languages and OS commands.

### Data Privacy & PII

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never disable TLS/SSL verification
NEVER use `rejectUnauthorized: false`, `verify=False`, or `InsecureSkipVerify: true`. Always enforce certificate validation in HTTP clients and database connections. For local dev, use mkcert for self-signed certificates. Disabling verification allows MITM attacks.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit
MUST use TLS 1.2+ for all network communication and AES-256 for storage. Hash passwords with bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2—never MD5/SHA. NEVER implement custom crypto—use crypto, libsodium, or equivalent established libraries. Example: `bcrypt.hash(password, 10)` for password storage.

### Authorization & Access Control

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Authorization on every endpoint
ALWAYS verify server-side that authenticated user can perform action on the specific resource. Check ownership on `/:id` routes and role on admin endpoints. Example: `if(resource.userId !== auth.userId) throw Forbidden()`. Never rely on client-side guards alone.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never implement custom authentication
NEVER build custom password hashing, JWT validation, session management, or OAuth flows. MUST use the project's established auth system or proven provider (Auth0, Firebase Auth, Cognito, Clerk). Custom auth has timing attacks, weak token generation, improper invalidation. Use existing.

### Destructive Operations

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No unguarded destructive operations
NEVER execute DELETE, DROP, rm -rf, git push --force, or git reset --hard without explicit user confirmation. Require: confirmation flag, soft deletes over hard deletes, and tested rollback scripts. AI tools must never autonomously perform irreversible data loss operations.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Database changes through migrations only
MUST use project migrations (Drizzle/Prisma/Alembic/Flyway) for all schema changes. NEVER run direct ALTER TABLE or ad-hoc SQL. Every migration requires a rollback script. Direct changes bypass peer review and rollback capability.

### Error Exposure

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No internal details in error responses
NEVER return stack traces, SQL, file paths, or internal IDs in API responses. MUST use centralized error handler returning: `{"error":{"code":"ERROR_CODE","message":"Safe message","requestId":"..."}}`. Map all exceptions through handler before responding to client.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### PII in logs
NEVER log, expose in API responses, or store in queries: email, phone, SSN, payment card, password, auth tokens, or user IDs. Mask or hash sensitive data. Example: log 'user_id=hash(12345)' not 'user_id=12345'. PII exposure enables identity theft and violates compliance (GDPR, PCI).

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### secrets management
NEVER commit secrets, API keys, or credentials to code. MUST use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., .env, AWS Secrets Manager). Example: `process.env.DATABASE_URL`, not hardcoded connection strings. Scan commits for exposed secrets immediately.

## Architecture & Patterns

### Error Handling

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No silent error swallowing
NEVER use empty catch blocks, bare `except: pass`, or `.catch(() => {})`. Every catch MUST handle (retry/fallback/message), re-throw, or log with context. If safe to ignore, document WHY in a comment. Example: `catch(e) { logger.error('User signup failed', {userId, error: e.message}); throw e; }`

### Testing

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Tests required for new logic
MUST write tests for all new functions, endpoints, and behavioral changes. Minimum: happy path test + one error case + one boundary condition. Use the project's existing test framework and location (e.g., `__tests__/` or `.test.ts` files). Example: if adding a user validation function, test valid input, invalid email format, and empty string.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### input validation
MUST validate all inputs (query, body, path) with Zod before processing. Example: `const schema = z.object({reference_id: z.string().uuid()}); schema.parse(req.query);` Reject invalid inputs with 400 status code.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### repository pattern
NEVER call Prisma directly in service code. MUST use repository classes (e.g., `WebhookRepository`, `ChargeRepository`) for all database access. Example: `const webhook = await webhookRepo.findById(id)` not `prisma.webhook.findUnique()`.

## Conventions

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### structured logging
Log all events with structured JSON: `{timestamp, level, service, userId, action, result, error}`. Never log PII directly; use IDs instead. Example: `{level: 'error', action: 'auth_failed', userId: 'u123', reason: 'invalid_token'}`

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### tailwind css usage
NEVER use inline styles or CSS-in-JS. ALWAYS use Tailwind CSS classes only. Example: `className="fixed inset-0 bg-black/50 flex items-center justify-center"` not `style={{position: 'fixed'}}`. See `.cursorrules` for Tailwind setup.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### error response format
ALWAYS return errors as `{ error: { code: "ERROR_CODE", message: "human description" } }`. Example: `{ error: { code: "MISSING_PARAMETER", message: "reference_id is required" } }`. Never return raw stack traces or unstructured error text.
